name: AlgoBlock
short_description: A set of physically connectable command blocks that children assemble by hand to build Logo-like programs, designed to support collaborative learning.
long_description: AlgoBlock, presented by Hideyuki Suzuki and Hiroshi Kato at the first International Conference on Computer Support for Collaborative Learning (CSCL 1995), is a set of graspable physical blocks that each correspond to a command in a Logo-like language; learners snap the blocks together on a shared work surface to construct a program that drives an on-screen submarine. By making the program a manipulable physical artifact placed in shared space, it deliberately turns coding into a visible, jointly accessible activity that stimulates conversation and cooperation among learners. It is widely credited as an early instance of tangible programming and a foundational example of tangible user interfaces, where computation is represented and manipulated through physical objects rather than only screen symbols.
ai_note: AlgoBlock is a tangible programming interface where physical blocks map to fixed Logo-like commands; there is no machine learning or AI inference in the system.

images.caption: Learners collaboratively assembling AlgoBlock's connectable command blocks to build a program.
images.alt_text: Photograph of people gathered around a table connecting rectangular physical command blocks together to form a tangible computer program.

interactions.title: Drive the submarine by snapping commands together
interactions.description: Children physically connect command blocks in a row on the shared table to assemble a Logo-like program, then run it to steer an on-screen submarine through a maze toward a goal.

interactions.title: Dial in how far it goes
interactions.description: Blocks like FORWARD carry a parameter switch the learner turns to set a value, so tuning the submarine's distance or turn is a physical adjustment on the block itself rather than a typed number.

interactions.title: Loop and branch with control blocks
interactions.description: Repeat-until and if-then-else blocks let learners build loops and conditionals out of the same graspable pieces, structuring a real program by hand instead of on a screen.

interactions.title: Watch the program light up block by block
interactions.description: As the program runs, a lamp on each block flashes in turn, so learners can literally see the flow of control travel down the row of blocks and connect a misbehaving submarine to the block that caused it.

interactions.title: Step through a bug by pressing buttons
interactions.description: In debug mode learners trace the program one command at a time by pushing a button on each block, watching the submarine inch forward so they can localise where the logic goes wrong.

interactions.title: Point at the blocks instead of describing them
interactions.description: Because the program sits in shared physical space, the authors observed children pointing directly at and grabbing blocks while talking, using abridged deictic speech and each other's eye lines and gestures to coordinate who changes what.

interactions.title: Hand a teammate the loop block
interactions.description: Because each command is a separate graspable object, a group could split a task by passing physical blocks around the table — one child owns the turn, another the repeat — making division of labour in coding a literal exchange of pieces.

interactions.title: Race two submarines from rival block programs
interactions.description: Two teams each assemble their own block sequence and run them against the same maze, turning the trace-the-lamps debugging loop into a side-by-side contest over whose program reaches the goal in fewer moves.

sources.title: Interaction-level support for collaborative learning: AlgoBlock — an open programming language
